name: Questions with ka
summary: Make any polite sentence a question by adding the particle か (ka) at the end. No word-order change needed.
explanation:
Asking questions in Japanese is delightfully simple. Take a polite statement and add か (ka) to the end. 学生です (gakusei desu, you are a student) becomes 学生ですか (gakusei desu ka, are you a student?). The word order does not change at all, unlike English which flips the subject and verb.

The か turns the sentence into a yes/no question. To answer, use はい (hai, yes) or いいえ (iie, no). For question-word questions, you place the question word - 何 (nani/nan, what), どこ (doko, where), 誰 (dare, who), いつ (itsu, when) - in the natural slot and still finish with か. 何を食べますか (nani o tabemasu ka) means 'What do you eat?'.

In casual speech, the か is often dropped and a rising intonation does the work instead: 食べる? (taberu?) means 'Will you eat?'. But in polite Japanese, keep the か. Written questions in Japanese often end with か rather than a question mark.

examples.native: これはあなたの本ですか。(kore wa anata no hon desu ka.)
コーヒーを飲みますか。(koohii o nomimasu ka.)
お名前は何ですか。(onamae wa nan desu ka.)
駅はどこですか。(eki wa doko desu ka.)
いつ来ますか。(itsu kimasu ka.)
誰がこれを作りましたか。(dare ga kore o tsukurimashita ka.)

commonMistakes: Changing the word order English-style. Japanese keeps the order and just adds か.
Adding a question mark instead of か in polite writing. Japanese questions end in か.
Forgetting that 何 is read 'nan' before です and 'nani' before を (nan desu ka, nani o).
Dropping か in polite speech. The casual rising-intonation question is for friends, not polite contexts.
Putting the question word at the front and reordering. Keep it in its natural slot.
tip: One little か at the end turns any polite sentence into a question - the order never changes.
